LinkedIn respects your privacy

LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

  • Top Content

  • Learning

  • Games
Sign in Join now

How to use "Easy Apply" on LinkedIn without hurting your chances.

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Corey Fleitz
1y
  • Report this post

Using “Easy Apply” on LinkedIn is a quick way to get your profile in front of people like me. Take my word: it will 100% hurt your chances of landing an interview *IF* your LinkedIn profile isn’t fully built out or aligned with your resume. When TA professionals/Recruiters (or even hiring managers) review applications, we rely on the details in your profile to assess your experience. Seems like an obvious statement. Because the vast majority of LI profiles I review lack key information, or worse, don’t match a resume, I have no real context to properly evaluate you. If I can't properly evaluate your experience, you're probably not moving forward in the interview process. 🫠 If “Easy Apply” on LinkedIn is your jam, take the time to: ✔ Ensure your LinkedIn profile mirrors your resume, including job titles, responsibilities, and achievements. Also, please please please take the time and fill out key sections like about me, work experience, and skills. ✔ Attach your actual resume when submitting your profile.

171 39 Comments
Like Comment
♦️ Anna Miller 1y
  • Report this comment

This is so true. Easy Apply without a complete and aligned profile is just throwing your resume into a void. If your profile doesn’t clearly tell your story and back it up with details, it’s hard for anyone to see the full picture. Thanks for breaking this down clearly.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Invivify 1y
  • Report this comment

We tell our clients to apply directly through company websites and specifically not through LinkedIn due to the number dated and junk/scam jobs that are posted. We also advise them to keep their profile a bit wide and really dial it in on the resume. Reviewing your profile as an example, I'd want to see the written content slimmed down a bit with more a focus on quantification, including how your work aligns with the company growth strategy. As a Hiring Manager, I might also be wondering if you had a side-gig going on while working full time, which might be a concern depending on the company.

Like
Reply
1 Reaction 2 Reactions

More Relevant Posts

  • Sumaiya .
    8mo
    • Report this post

    🚀 Stop Searching Endlessly — Start Getting Hired! If you’ve been applying to countless jobs without results, it’s time to change your strategy. Here are 14 proven steps that can help you boost your interview chances and land your dream job faster. 1. Tailor Every Resume Customize your resume for each job. Highlight key skills that align with the job description and adjust your summary to reflect the company’s goals. 2. Craft a Powerful Summary Your professional summary should clearly describe your strengths, achievements, and how you fit the role — in just 3–4 lines. 3. Decode Job Descriptions Analyze the job post carefully. Identify what skills and qualities the employer truly values. 4. Upgrade Resume Points Start each bullet point with a strong action verb and show measurable results. Example: Improved website performance by 40% through optimized code. 5. Build an Impressive LinkedIn Profile Use a professional photo, a clear headline, and a well-written “About” section that highlights your skills and goals. 6. Present Your Skills Confidently Show how your skills created real impact in your past work or projects. 7. Prepare Smartly for Interviews Research the company, understand its mission, and align your answers with their objectives. 8. Know Common Interview Questions Practice answering the top 10 most frequently asked questions for your target role. 9. Network with Purpose When connecting with professionals, express genuine interest and mention how you can contribute or learn — not just job requests. 10. Match Resume with Job Description Before applying, check how well your resume matches the job requirements. Aim for at least 80% relevance. 11. Write Professional Emails Keep your emails short and polite. Highlight your achievements and express genuine enthusiasm for the role. 12. Prepare Role-Based Questions Ask insightful questions during interviews about the team, goals, and growth opportunities. 13. Perfect Your Self-Introduction Briefly mention your background, key skills, and what excites you about the position. 14. Follow Up Professionally After the interview, send a short thank-you email and politely inquire about the status of your application. If you found this helpful: ✅ Follow Sumaiya for more valuable career insights 👍 Like & Repost — it might help someone in need

    66 17 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Sergey Butyrskiy
    9mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Searching for a job in 2025 turned out to be harder than doing one. Some lessons after 100+ rejections and 2 offers. Job search nowadays is a full-time job itself. Being in sales for years, I thought I knew everything about searching for customers — Apply, wait, talk, smile. At the end of the day, it’s just a sales pitch. But it turned out to be more nuanced and took time to understand how it works. The points below look to me like an absolute must for job search in 2025, while many weren’t even relevant a few years ago: 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗼 𝗺𝗮𝘆 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗺𝗲. Generally, people are way more open to providing referrals than I originally thought. Now, AI is writing resumes and screening them on the other side. A human emotional touch becomes a must to stand out. 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝘂𝗹𝗮𝗿 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻. Before they ask you that perfect interview question — you still have to earn the right to be asked, by breaking through 100+ applications, AI filters, ghosting, and rejections. Then comes the actual interview: 2 minutes to impress, smile, sound smart, overcome fear — and say everything just right. While a post has no competition, no pressure — and works 24/7. 𝗧𝗵𝗼𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗻𝘆𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴. Simple deep research about lesser-known company facts helps craft better, more insightful questions — and is usually recognized. 𝗕𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝘀𝗮𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗻𝗼. There are just tons of ways to say the same thing without saying "no". 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘀𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗯𝗲𝗴𝗴𝗮𝗿 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗻𝗲𝗿. Step out of the weak “beggar” position — which we default to under pressure — and into a stronger one. Despite being neglectful and non-responsive, companies actually do desperately need you. 𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗥 - most typical questions are the same and answers can be prepared in advance using STAR and well-rehearsed. 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘇𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆. I prioritized high-quality, personalized replies over mass applications with AI-automated resume tailoring. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗲 Completeness and the right keywords help you get noticed by LinkedIn algorithms and rank higher in search. It just creates some inbound traffic and opportunities. 𝗙𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄-𝘂𝗽 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀. Thank-you emails, relevant LinkedIn posts, background stories, use cases — it all helps. Share your tips in the comments. How do you approach the search?

  • Ahmed D
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Spending $500 on a resume rewrite? Most of the time, you don’t need to. Because here’s the truth: What recruiters want isn’t a “fancy” resume It’s one that proves results. And with the right ChatGPT prompts, you can write that yourself. That’s why I built this carousel: 👉 10 ChatGPT Prompts To Upgrade Your Resume More Than a $500 Rewrite Turn vague bullets into metric-driven wins Quantify scope, scale, and results Make your resume stand out in any ATS Don’t pay for fluff. Use AI to show your real achievements. Swipe through and tell me Have you tried ChatGPT for your resume yet?

    1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Edikan Peter
    9mo
    • Report this post

    MY FRIEND APPLIED TO 45 JOBS. RECEIVED NO RESPONSE. No interviews. No callbacks. Just crickets. Then we ran his resume through ChatGPT… 8 responses in a week. Here are the 7 prompts that flipped everything: 👇 1 | Rewrite My Resume Like a Recruiter Prompt: “You’re a hiring manager. Rewrite my resume for a [specific job role], using measurable results and attention-grabbing phrasing.” 2 | Make It ATS-Proof Prompt: “Optimize my resume so it’s fully Applicant Tracking System-friendly. Include keywords relevant to [job title] for 2025.” 3 | Tear It Apart (So It’s Better) Prompt: “Give me blunt, no-filter feedback on my resume. What’s weak, what’s okay, and how can I make it stand out instantly?” 4 | Craft a Cover Letter That Pops Prompt: “Write me a personalized cover letter for [job title] that tells my story, highlights passion, and separates me from other applicants.” 5 | Tailor It to the Job Prompt: “Take this job posting and adjust my resume so it matches point-by-point, using strong, data-backed language.” 6 | Fix My Resume Gaps Prompt: “Rewrite my resume to downplay employment gaps by emphasizing transferable skills, growth, and impact.” 7 | Build a LinkedIn Bio That Gets Noticed Prompt: “Write a LinkedIn bio that positions me as professional, approachable, and hire-ready, even if I’m switching fields.” Save this.

    4
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Thom Gibson
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Not even getting interviews for jobs you're highly qualified for? Here’s the strategy that finally got me hired: I stopped applying like everyone else. Instead of sending another resume into the void, I created a short video showing I could do the job. The unexpected turn of events? I was planning on submitting my application before I published the video on LinkedIn but the listing closed before I could even apply. I thought 'Argh, I just spent 3 days on this video and application and now I can't even apply!?' But I posted it anways. And the video made its way through the company’s Slack, landed in front of the hiring manager, and got me the interview. A week later, I had an offer in hand. This works even if you’re not applying for a social media role. My friend used the same approach to showcase his AI sales workflow. His video didn’t go viral. It just reached the right people. Yes, he could have put the same video in his portfolio but that's so much easier for hiring teams to miss. If you want to stand out in a sea of AI-generated resumes: → Showcase what you know publicly. → Tag the people you want to work with. → Let your work speak louder than your application. Good luck out there!

    95 61 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Mahmoud Youssef
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Yesterday one of our team members spent 42 minutes on a single company before even clicking “apply.” Not to rewrite the resume. Not to chase an opening. Just to confirm whether the actual hiring manager still worked there. That’s the part no one sees. The research rabbit hole before the first application even leaves our screen. We’ll dig through old press releases, check LinkedIn activity, scan team pages, even peek at Glassdoor timelines to spot who got promoted last month. Sometimes we realize the role was filled before it was posted. Other times, we find a hidden one that hasn’t been listed yet. Most job seekers don’t have the bandwidth for that level of digging. They apply to 50+ roles in one sitting, cross their fingers, and wonder why it goes silent. We apply to maybe 10. But every one of those 10 gets a resume that mirrors the company’s own language, a message that lands in the right inbox, and timing that aligns with when recruiters are actually checking applications. That’s why a “slow week” here still gets more callbacks than most people get in a month. One client told us, “I didn’t realize how different it feels when someone is actually behind the application.” It’s not fancy software or AI magic — it’s manual, strategic, and very human. Curious what part of this process you’d want to try on your own? Drop it below, I’ll share how we do it step by step.

    1
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Mukesh Gupta
    8mo
    • Report this post

    ❌ No replies. No interviews. Just silence for 90 days. Then, I ran my resume through ChatGPT. Got five callbacks in less than a week-using just six prompts. [Save for later] 1. Spot the Flaws Prompt: Act as a recruiter for [your industry/role]. Review my resume below and highlight weak areas, overused buzzwords, and missing metrics. Be brutally honest. 2. Rewrite for Impact Prompt: Rewrite this resume to sound more results-driven, quantifiable, and compelling for [target role]. Focus on achievements, not just duties. 3. ATS Boost Prompt: Update this resume to be fully optimized for Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) for the role of [specific role/title]. Use industry-specific keywords naturally. 4. Craft My Hook Prompt: Write a powerful, 3-line professional summary that hooks a recruiter in under 10 seconds. Prioritize impact, clarity, and value. 5. Upgrade Experience Prompt: Rephrase the experience section to highlight impact, results, and transferable skills using action verbs and quantifiable outcomes. 6. Format Fix Prompt: Suggest a clean, modern resume format that works for both humans and ATS. No graphics, no columns. Just structured and effective. 🔖 BONUS 🚀 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗿𝗮 𝗣𝗹𝘂𝘀 𝗔𝗻𝗻𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿: – 𝗨𝗻𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝟭𝟬,𝟬𝟬𝟬+ 𝗷𝗼𝗯-𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝘀 – 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗴𝗹𝗲, 𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗮, 𝗜𝗕𝗠 & 𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀 👉 𝗘𝗻𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗹 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲: https://lnkd.in/dn_sWYfx 🐦🔥 Google Data Analytics Certificate: 🔗https://lnkd.in/dybz8i67 🐦🔥Google's Python Programming Course 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dVmfpw4D 🐦🔥Google Generative Al 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dNQbeysr 🐦🔥Google IT Support Professional Certificate 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dWuPsHPZ 🐦🔥Google IT Automation with Python 🔗 https://lnkd.in/d5Fexb-b 🐦🔥Reliable Google Cloud Infrastructure: Design and Process 🔗 https://lnkd.in/duzbS5p6 🐦🔥Preparing for Google Cloud Certification 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dD2avK2V 🐦🔥 Google UX Design Professional Certificate 🔗 https://lnkd.in/d4MGaWGG 🐦🔥Google Cybersecurity Professional Certificate 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dShWgJYj 🐦🔥Google Ads Certifications 🔗 https://lnkd.in/d7eEFPqM 🐦🔥Google Project Management Professional Certificate 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dJSA4PWH 🐦🔥Google Professional Workspace Administrator 🔗 https://lnkd.in/dKJHNswP 𝗛𝗮𝗽𝗽𝘆 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 💯

  • Rajat Gajbhiye
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Give me 2 minutes, I’ll tell you how to get interview calls ➤ 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲 𝘁𝗶𝗽𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱: → Each bullet should start with a strong action word(Built, Designed, Optimized, Integrated…) → Don’t repeat action words. If every line starts with “Worked on”, it looks copy-pasted → Every bullet should answer: What you built + what tech you used + what impact it had → Only 4 sections needed: Experience, Projects, Skills, Education → Backend roles? System design & API, backend tech → Frontend roles? Highlight UI performance + user features 𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝗲𝗱 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲: → https://lnkd.in/gXcPGdif ➤ 𝗡𝗮𝘂𝗸𝗿𝗶 𝗧𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗸𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱: → Create multiple Naukri profiles targeting different locations. → Use a different phone number and email address for each account. → Update your job profile every morning. → Include all relevant keywords for your job role in your resume. → Add those same keywords in the resume headline section. → Set your notice period to 15 days or less. → Use 6-7 bullet points to describe your each projects. → Follow a similar bullet-point format for the Employment section. → Add your LinkedIn profile link. → Include all job locations in the Preferred Work Location field. → Add any relevant certifications you have. → Replace career gaps with freelance work in your resume. Repost it to help others!♻️

    152 18 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Dawn Brunn CPC, MEd.
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Good news post! After 8 mos of searching, applying online to over 50 roles (i.e. using ResumeWorded ATS optimization to target each resume to each role), attending 15 different networking groups, and going on 20 informational interviews - one of my clients accepted a job offer today!!!! How did they do it? They did it the old-fashioned way with the old, “do you like where you work?” strategy. Since less than 2% of resumes get selected from an online database, strategic networking is still essential. AND letting friends, family, neighbors people standing in line at the grocery store, etc.. know that you looking for a new job. So, how did my client do it? He was at a random happy hour and the person standing next to him was waiting for their order of wings….. So, my client decided to strike up a conversation and then asked these three questions. 1. Where do you work? 2. Do you like it? 3. Are you hiring? In this instance they were hiring! My client participated in two rounds of interviews, and was an extended an offer in less than two weeks. After eight months of searching - a simple conversation (at a random happy hour) helped him land his next big adventure. Admittedly, it’s a tough job market, and with advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and robots scanning resumes for key words - it has become increasingly more difficult to land in interview, therefore knowledge sharing is paramount. Please comment below if you have a unique story as to how you landed your recent job. I believe the more we share this good news, and our job-seeker success stories - the more we can help each other find creative ways to land jobs in this market, and so it is!

    No replies. No interviews. Just silence for 90 days. Then, I ran my resume through ChatGPT. Got 5 callbacks in less than a week-using just 6 prompts. [Save for later] 1. Spot the Flaws Prompt: Act as a recruiter for [your industry/role]. Review my resume below and highlight weak areas, overused buzzwords, and missing metrics. Be brutally honest. 2. Rewrite for Impact Prompt: Rewrite this resume to sound more results-driven, quantifiable, and compelling for [target role]. Focus on achievements, not just duties. 3. ATS Boost Prompt: Update this resume to be fully optimized for Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) for the role of [specific role/title]. Use industry-specific keywords naturally. 4. Craft My Hook Prompt: Write a powerful, 3-line professional summary that hooks a recruiter in under 10 seconds. Prioritize impact, clarity, and value. 5. Upgrade Experience Prompt: Rephrase the experience section to highlight impact, results, and transferable skills using action verbs and quantifiable outcomes. 6. Format Fix Prompt: Suggest a clean, modern resume format that works for both humans and ATS. No graphics, no columns. 𝗙𝗥𝗘𝗘 (𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗴𝗹𝗲) 𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘁 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱. 1. Google Digital Marketing & E-commerce: → https://lnkd.in/gZrc4Fnv 8. Google IT Support: → https://lnkd.in/gEaB6FzQ 9. Web Applications for Everybody Specialization: → https://lnkd.in/gshfFzBk 10. Data Analysis with R Programming: → https://lnkd.in/gk2Ny2M3 14. IBM Full Stack Software Developer Professional Certificate: → https://lnkd.in/gsJhz6sQ 15.
